title: Gout & Pseudogout: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Gout & Pseudogout
Identify key diagnostic features of Gout & Pseudogout
Apply clinical reasoning to Gout & Pseudogout management
Recognize common pitfalls in Gout & Pseudogout

sections.title: Overview of Gout & Pseudogout
sections.content:
**Gout & Pseudogout**

Monosodium urate vs calcium pyrophosphate, crystal analysis, acute management, urate-lowering therapy

**Key Points:**
• Gout: monosodium urate (MSU) crystals — needle-shaped, negatively birefringent (yellow parallel)
• Pseudogout (CPPD): calcium pyrophosphate — rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent (blue parallel)
• Gout: 1st MTP (podagra) classic; Pseudogout: knee most common

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Gout: monosodium urate (MSU) crystals — needle-shaped, negatively birefringent (yellow parallel)
• Pseudogout (CPPD): calcium pyrophosphate — rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent (blue parallel)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Gout: 1st MTP (podagra) classic; Pseudogout: knee most common
• Acute gout treatment: NSAIDs, colchicine, or corticosteroids (NOT allopurinol during acute flare)
• Urate-lowering: allopurinol or febuxostat (start after 2 flares, tophi, or CKD with urate >9)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Gout & Pseudogout:**

• Gout: 1st MTP (podagra) classic; Pseudogout: knee most common
• Acute gout treatment: NSAIDs, colchicine, or corticosteroids (NOT allopurinol during acute flare)
• Urate-lowering: allopurinol or febuxostat (start after 2 flares, tophi, or CKD with urate >9)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Gout & Pseudogout?

options.text: Gout: monosodium urate (MSU) crystals — needle-shaped, negatively birefringent (yellow parallel)
options.explanation: Gout: monosodium urate (MSU) crystals — needle-shaped, negatively birefringent (yellow parallel)

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Gout & Pseudogout?

options.text: Urate-lowering: allopurinol or febuxostat (start after 2 flares, tophi, or CKD with urate >9)
options.explanation: Urate-lowering: allopurinol or febuxostat (start after 2 flares, tophi, or CKD with urate >9)

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Gout & Pseudogout. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Pseudogout (CPPD): calcium pyrophosphate — rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent (blue parallel)
options.explanation: Pseudogout (CPPD): calcium pyrophosphate — rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent (blue parallel)

mcqs.stem: A 65-year-old woman with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and ESR of 85 mm/hr is suspected of having giant cell arteritis. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Start high-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) immediately, then arrange temporal artery biopsy
options.explanation: Per ACR/EULAR guidelines, GCA with visual symptoms is an emergency. High-dose glucocorticoids must be started IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic even after several weeks of steroid therapy due to persistent inflammatory changes.

options.text: Schedule temporal artery biopsy first to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
options.explanation: Waiting for biopsy before treatment risks permanent vision loss. Biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without significantly affecting sensitivity.

options.text: Start low-dose prednisone (10 mg/day) and monitor symptoms for 1 week
options.explanation: Low-dose steroids are insufficient for GCA. The recommended dose is 40-60 mg/day for uncomplicated GCA, or 1 mg/kg/day (up to IV pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days) when visual symptoms are present.

options.text: Order CRP and IL-6 levels before making treatment decisions
options.explanation: While CRP and IL-6 are elevated in GCA, additional testing should not delay treatment in a patient with classic symptoms and elevated ESR. Vision loss can occur within hours.
